data.title: Lansing Street Advisors Q1 2026 Letter
data.description:
All Along the Watchtower

Song by The Jimi Hendrix Experience (1968)

“There must be some kind of way out of here”

Said the joker to the thief

“There’s too much confusion I can’t get no relief

Businessmen, they drink my wine Plowmen dig my earth

None will level on the line

Nobody offered his word”

Introduction

“There’s too much confusion, I can’t get no relief.” Hendrix wasn’t writing about global markets, but he might as well have been. War in Iran, political turmoil, an AI reckoning, and a private credit crisis are dominating headlines all at once and investors are searching for a way out. But through the noise, American business keeps doing what it does best: innovating and producing higher earnings.

In my year-end 2025 letter, we discussed stock market volatility versus bear markets, with the expectation that 2026 would bring heightened turbulence. That expectation has arrived in force. Although the Iran conflict is front and center, the broader thesis from that letter remains intact. As the chart below shows, 100% of midterm election years since 1950 have produced significant corrections, averaging more than –17%.

Add the well-documented pattern of –10%+ pullbacks surrounding the appointment of a new Fed chair, and 2026 is delivering both events in a single year.

The good news: one-year returns following midterm correction troughs have been positive every single time.

The year-end letter also introduced “the law of unpredictability”—which turned out to be accidentally perfect timing heading into the 2026 bombing of Iran. Markets are amoral. Wars tend to produce short-term disruptions that, historically, create buying opportunities. As the chart below illustrates, geopolitical shocks have rarely derailed markets for long. That may prove true again—but today’s letter will examine the three trends I believe will define the balance of 2026.

Part I. Rising Prices and Rising Rates After the Bombs Drop

The Iran conflict triggered the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, and that single event sent commodities sharply higher. Oil grabbed the headlines, but other critical supply chains—shipping, fertilizer, chemicals—have also been disrupted. Just when the Fed appeared ready to cut rates, a geopolitical curveball sent interest rates in the opposite direction.

The charts below paint a picture of rising prices hitting Americans where it hurts most. This comes at a time when affordability is the number one political topic in the country—and more importantly, it is pushing the 10- year Treasury yield higher.

Shipping, Fertilizer, and Chemical Price Increases

Right now, Americans are feeling the pain at the gas pump, in the grocery store, and through higher borrowing costs. But international markets are in a full-blown supply chain crisis. Unleaded gas is up roughly 35%.

Fertilizer and shipping costs are driving food prices higher. Mortgage rates remain stubbornly elevated.

For all you Millennials and Gen Z readers—do not look now, but energy stocks are outperforming technology stocks on a one-year basis. As the chart below shows, energy is closing in on three-year outperformance. This may dissipate quickly if the conflict resolves, but oil prices are unlikely to reset to recent lows under any uneasy peace agreement.

XLE Energy ETF (Purple) vs. XLK Tech ETF (Orange)

The biggest market consequence of the war is that the Fed is now frozen, with almost no chance of rate cuts in 2026. In fact, as the chart below shows, there is now a higher probability of a rate hike than a cut.

Probability of a Rate Hike Higher Than a Cut (as of March 2026)

Part II. Private Credit and Private Equity

“There is never just one cockroach.” —Jamie Dimon, JPMorgan

Rising interest rates and a frozen Fed are the perfect segue into a more dangerous market condition: the private credit and private equity sector. These industries experienced explosive growth coming out of the 2008 financial crisis, stepping in where banks pulled back from lending to small businesses. They have been one of the great American financial success stories of the last fifteen years. Now they are facing their first real test.

Let me be clear at the outset: Lansing Street Advisors would not exist without private equity. A good portion of our clients built their wealth by selling companies to private equity funds. Private credit and equity have delivered massive liquidity to small business owners across the country while generating strong returns for fund shareholders. But like any sector, they will experience bull and bear markets. This is their first full-blown sector bear market—and the question is how far the damage will spread.

I am going to do my best to keep this at an elevated level and not lose readers in the weeds. Two definitions:

Private credit is debt financing provided directly by non-bank lenders to companies through privately negotiated loans. Think of it as non-bank loans.

Private equity is where funds acquire or take stakes in private companies, improve their operations, and sell them for a profit. Think of it as funds taking equity stakes in small businesses.

Right now, the crisis is concentrated in private lending, but the risk of it spreading to private equity is part of the broader apprehension. According to Pitchbook, private credit has tripled since 2015 to $1.3 trillion. The Financial Times puts total private loans at $2 trillion by the end of 2025.

In 2025, two large defaults—Tricolor Holdings and FirstBrands Group—ignited fear across the sector. Both companies sought bankruptcy protection amid accusations of fraudulent bookkeeping. These cases raised serious red flags about the level of underwriting and due diligence in the private lending industry. As Jamie Dimon has said: “There is never just one cockroach.”

Institutional investors—pension funds, endowments, hedge funds, and insurance companies—own an estimated 70% of private loans, with the remaining 30% held by high-net-worth individuals.

Here is the next key point to understand: private credit is “semi-liquid,” typically capping quarterly withdrawals at 5%. You cannot press a key on your computer and sell your holdings. If withdrawal requests exceed the 5% cap, investors get prorated and must resubmit the following quarter. As the charts below show, withdrawal requests have increased—sparking worries about a classic “run on the bank.”

Speaking of banks: the private lending business has been so lucrative that traditional banks have made roughly $300 billion in loans to private credit companies. Yes, banks lending to companies that lend to companies. The good news is that U.S. bank balance sheets are in solid shape and $300 billion in exposure is manageable—but this daisy chain reinforces the broader credit-crisis narrative.

On top of banks, insurance companies have approximately 25% of their investment portfolios in private lending. Pensions and endowments hold up to 30% in illiquid private equity and debt. Trillions in private funds, public bank exposure, life insurance exposure, institutional money exposure.

This is why the alarming phrase “credit crisis” is back in the headlines. We have not had a credit crisis in the United States since 2008. A credit crisis freezes lending and liquidity across the economy as defaults and loss of confidence feed on each other.

This leads to the big question: what is the current default rate? Fitch reports 9.2% for 2025—high enough to leave investors worried about being the last one out the door. We have wood to chop before this is over.

Exacerbating the problem are three structural factors:

Illiquidity. Publicly traded private funds are semi-liquid with quarterly withdrawal caps. Non-publicly traded funds have no set liquidity dates whatsoever.

Opaqueness. Private managers lack the disclosure requirements of public companies. “Mark-to-market” pricing on loan values and company valuations is self-reported. When you grade your own homework, investors get nervous.

Complexity. Here are just some of the strategies being executed by private equity firms: secondaries, continuation funds, NAV lending, preferred equity, strip sales, sale of GP stakes, and PIK loans. Insurance companies with large private investments run offshore reinsurance affiliates domiciled in Bermuda or the Cayman Islands, adding layers of regulatory complexity.

The final blow to private lending in late 2025 was the repricing of the technology sector—especially what some are calling the “SAAS apocalypse,” where AI tools began disrupting traditional software-as-a-service subscription models that were once considered toll-booth businesses with bulletproof recurring revenue.

Private credit lenders have 25–30% exposure to software companies, and AI’s potential impact on this sub- sector has made investors skeptical that anyone can reliably model future cash flows or valuations. More on this in Part III.

If the private world goes “worst case”—are we facing a 2008 situation?

I think not.

As Torsten Slok at Apollo has pointed out, the direct lending market is roughly $2 trillion—representing about 3% of total debt outstanding for U.S. households and businesses. By comparison, mortgages accounted for approximately 60% of total household and corporate debt at the peak of the housing bubble in 2006. Three percent versus sixty percent. The scale is simply not comparable.

Private equity and credit filled a void of liquidity after the 2008 crisis. Now they are facing their first sector-level credit crunch. This will create winners and losers, but America is a dynamic marketplace—when these loans take bigger markdowns, opportunistic investors will line up to buy them at a discount. That’s how markets work.

The questions remain: how far will this affect the real economy, and will it spread beyond the private sector?

Right now, loan growth at banks remains strong, credit spreads are still tight, and there are no signs of a systemic crisis materializing. But we are in the early innings of a private lending bear market, and prudence is recommended.

Part III. The Revaluation of the Technology Sector

Here is a statistic that would have seemed impossible two years ago: technology stocks’ forward P/E multiples now equal the defensive consumer staples sector. In 2021, the Magnificent 7 traded at roughly 40 times earnings versus 20 times for staples. Today, that gap has closed entirely.

Technology sub-sectors are in bear markets—not corrections. The distinction matters.

Software (IGV): down 33%. Cybersecurity (CIBR): down 20%. SaaS (XSW): down 33%. Magnificent 7 (MAGS): down 17.5%. A number of well-known retail trading favorites in these sub-sectors have been cut in half.

This all started in October 2025 with the beginning of the SaaS and crypto repricing, when AI tools demonstrated their potential as a genuine threat to the software-as-a-service subscription model. At the same time, the market grew skeptical of hyper-scalers’ massive AI spending without evidence of immediate revenue growth.

AI remains a “known unknown.” The market does not yet know who the winners and losers will be. But rather than wait around to find out, investors have chosen to de-risk first and ask questions later. That de-risking has played out as a broad technology trading slump—and it feeds directly into the private credit problem discussed in Part II, where lenders have heavy exposure to the very software companies being repriced.

Conclusion

The S&P 500 has compounded at a mid-teens annual rate for the 17 years from the trough of March 2009 through the close of 2025. In that period, the world witnessed roughly 200 conflicts and 10 to 15 major wars.

As the chart below shows, the S&P 500 has delivered a positive one-year return following virtually every major geopolitical event in modern history.

Right now, I rank the Iran war as #3 on the 2026 risk scale—behind the private equity/credit bear market at #2 and the “known unknown” of the AI buildout at #1. We expect volatility to remain elevated through 2026 due to midterm elections and the new Fed chair appointment.

Hendrix sang about confusion, about businessmen drinking his wine, about no one leveling on the line. That feels about right for 2026. There is genuine uncertainty in this market—more than we’ve seen in years. A war nobody predicted, a credit cycle nobody modeled, and a technology repricing nobody timed.

But confusion is not the same thing as catastrophe. And volatility is not the same thing as a bear market. Those distinctions matter—and understanding them is what separates investors from speculators.

The two riders approaching in Hendrix’s song could be anything—fear and opportunity, risk and reward, panic and patience. History tells us the investors who stay the course through periods of maximum confusion are the ones who come out ahead.

As always, stay the course. Investing is a psychology game, not an IQ game.

All Along the Watchtower

Song by The Jimi Hendrix Experience (1968)

All along the watchtower Princes kept the view

While all the women came and went Barefoot servants too

Outside in the cold distance A wildcat did growl

Two riders were approaching And the wind began to howl

data.title: Intel Proved Me Wrong - Now It Has To Prove The Market Right (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
When I last covered Intel Corporation (INTC) in late January of this year, I said the 13% pullback wasn’t a buy signal. In retrospect, I was wrong, as the stock is up over 33% since, outperforming the market, with the S&P 500 (SP500) actually down around 1.3% during the same period.

During this broader market meltdown as a result of the geopolitical uncertainty and supply chain disruptions, it seems as though Intel is holding up better than most. The stock is up around 34% over the past month, and up another 69% year to date. There aren't many names in tech that can make that claim right now. The semis, broadly, are getting worked, as TSMC (TSM) is navigating Taiwan Strait anxiety on top of the Hormuz supply chain disruption. Nvidia (NVDA) is caught between insatiable AI infrastructure demand and the uncomfortable reality that its most important customers, AKA hyperscalers building out Gulf-region data centers, are now operating in a war zone, and the list goes on.

So, Intel’s outperformance is a function of what Intel isn't: it isn't a pure-play AI accelerator story dependent on uninterrupted Pacific shipping lanes, nor is it priced to perfection. In a market that is actively repricing geopolitical concentration risk, Intel’s U.S. manufacturing footprint is suddenly looking less like a liability and more like a strategic asset. The irony is not lost on anyone who spent the last three years watching the market punish Intel for being too slow, too domestic, and too everything that TSMC wasn't.

Fund managers who spent Q1 overweight on TSMC and Nvidia are now sitting on geopolitical concentration risk they didn't model for, and Intel is one of the few names in the sector where that conversation doesn't apply, since you don't have to worry about a Taiwan Strait incident taking out your fab. Still, Intel continues doing the hard, slow, expensive work of rebuilding a manufacturing operation that should never have been allowed to fall behind in the first place, which makes me think that this run is standing on shaky grounds.

So, the question remains: why is Intel up 30% when the peers are struggling to have a green day?

Intel has spent the last six weeks quietly assembling a set of catalysts that nobody had fully priced in January. They next report their earnings on April 23rd. So, here's the rundown:

Why the market (suddenly) loves Intel:

Let’s start with April 1st. Intel announced it would repurchase Apollo Global Management's 49% equity stake in its Fab 34 facility in Leixlip, Ireland, for $14.2 billion, the same stake Apollo had originally acquired for $11.2 billion in 2024 when Intel needed outside capital to survive. The market took this positively, and rightfully so, as the stock jumped around 9% on the day, because a company buying back a strategic fab at a 27% premium to what it sold it for is not a company in distress. According to Intel’s CFO, David Zinser,

“Today, we have a stronger balance sheet, improved financial discipline, and an evolved business strategy. We appreciate Apollo’s continued collaboration to reach this outcome as we realign our capital structure with our long-term strategy.”

Then came Terafab. On April 7th, Intel announced it would partner with SpaceX (SPACE), Tesla (TSLA), and xAI (X.AI) on the Terafab project, a mega-fab in Austin designed to produce 1 terawatt of AI compute per year, with small-batch production expected in late 2026. According to Intel in an X post,

"Our ability to design, fabricate, and package ultra-high-performance chips at scale will help accelerate Terafab’s aim to produce 1 TW/year [terawatt per year] of compute to power future advances in AI and robotics.”

Having Musk's full ecosystem as a manufacturing customer provides something money cannot buy, which is basically reputational proof that Intel Foundry is a serious alternative to TSMC for cutting-edge AI silicon, or at least getting there. The stock was up another 3% on the announcement.

Then comes Google (GOOG)(GOOG). Intel deepened its partnership with Google to co-develop custom ASIC-based infrastructure processing units. According to Amin Vahdat, SVP & Chief Technologist, AI Infrastructure at Google,

“Intel has been a trusted partner for nearly two decades, and their Xeon roadmap gives us confidence that we can continue to meet the growing performance and efficiency demands of our workloads.”

The Google partnership is the one I am watching. Terafab is flashy and is a bet on the future, but Google is the present. Let’s think about what Google is telling its own procurement team with that statement. It's saying, we're comfortable committing to Intel's CPU roadmap for multiple generations of AI infrastructure build-out.

In an environment where hyperscalers are simultaneously building custom ASICs and AI accelerators specifically to reduce dependence on any single vendor, choosing to publicly reaffirm a multi-generational commitment to Intel's server roadmap is a meaningful signal, and the market took it as such, with the stock up another 5% on the news. This means the Xeon 6 is earning its place in Google's AI stack on performance merits.

Investment thesis:

When I said in January that the pullback wasn't a buy signal, I was looking at a company still burning cash on a foundry build, and still behind on process nodes, while sentiment ran through the roof, and most importantly, valuation ran ahead of fundamentals.

My argument for the sell was simple: the fundamentals hadn't changed; the market had just run too far ahead of them. The government taking a 10% stake in August had lit a fuse under the stock, and what I was watching for was real evidence. At the time, my takeaway was the following:

“What this quarter reinforced for me is that Intel’s story is still one of transition. There is real progress in data centers and AI, and the foundry narrative is directionally positive, but neither is yet strong enough to offset persistent weakness in PCs and margin compression. Soft guidance was the trigger, but the selloff was ultimately about valuation and positioning, not one quarter’s numbers. Intel continues to lose market share to AMD, and Panther Lake, though impressive, does not change the competitive reality in the near term. I think the stock will offer more attractive entry points in the mid-term, and would recommend investors to stay on the sidelines.”

Still, looking back, what I wasn't modeling was the velocity of the narrative shift. The geopolitical environment handed Intel a structural argument, onshore fabs, no Taiwan risk, CHIPS Act backing, and amid all that, the company didn’t stand still.

The company reports Q1 '26 on April 23rd. Intel guided for revenue of approximately $12.2 billion at the midpoint, an 11% sequential decline, with breakeven adjusted EPS and gross margins around 34.5%. The fundamentals are still messy, and the foundry segment is still unprofitable, but conventional screens aren't what's driving this. I was wrong in January, but the harder question now is whether I’m late to the party. With a lot of the positives priced in, what can really push the stock up from here?

My original thesis was a valuation call, not a fundamentals one, and that trade has played out. Now, while the narrative has shifted, I'm not chasing this at 69% year-to-date with breakeven EPS guidance and earnings carrying real downside risk if gross margins disappoint. The stock is trading well above where most analysts think it should be, but I’m upgrading the stock to a hold at current levels. The story is better than I thought, and the price reflects that. But until earnings later this month, I'm on the sidelines, just for different reasons than before.

Valuation and what’s next:

The stock is currently trading at $63, above all its EMAs, with EMA21 at $50, EMA50 AT $47, and EMA200 at $38. With RSI in overbought territory at 75, I would like to see the stock give back some of its gains, and enter the 1Q print with sentiment and fundamentals more aligned. The ideal entry for anyone not already in this is a pullback toward the $50-52 range, where the EMA21 provides support and the technical picture is less extended. But buying a 75 RSI into a quarter where revenue is expected to decline 11% sequentially is a risk that the risk-reward doesn't justify right now.

Forward P/E is at 125x, against a sector median of 21.6x, and forward EV/Sales is at 6.22x versus a sector median of 2.81x. The only honest way to defend these multiples is to argue that the forward estimates are depressed and will be revised materially upward as the foundry business scales and margins recover, but that's still a faith-based valuation.

Buying Intel at these levels is paying for what Intel might earn two or three years from now if 18A yields improve and 14A lands external customers. To keep a long story short, the margin for error is thin, and a lot is counting on Intel’s next print.

data.title: Atlassian: A Prime AI (Fear) Casualty (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Atlassian Corporation (TEAM) have been in an absolute freefall as of late, with investors being really concerned about the disruptive impact of AI on the business. A >$300 stock a little over a year ago has now fallen to lows in the fifties, levels first and last seen in 2018.

By now, sales multiples have collapsed to just over 2 times. Realistic break-even levels are in sight, and realistic profits are likely to be seen going forward thanks to recent cost-cutting efforts. By now, the valuation might be cheap enough to contemplate a position here, in the full understanding that this is a highly speculative position.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Solid Growth—Collapsing Stock

In February, Atlassian reported a 23% increase in second-quarter sales, that is, for the quarter that ended on December 31, 2025. Revenues were reported at $1.59 billion. Despite the strong growth and formidable revenue base, Atlassian still reported a GAAP operating loss of $47 million, down a little from the year before. In all fairness, GAAP earnings were depressed by a $25 million amortization charge, as otherwise there were some small, realistic losses reported.

The company reports large adjusted earnings, but these are very adjusted, including nearly half a billion dollars of stock-based compensation expense. The cash flow statement revealed that some $1.2 billion was spent on M&A during the quarter, following the completion of the deal for DX as well as The Browser Company of New York.

In part because of these deals, net cash holdings have come down to about half a billion here, and despite the GAAP losses reported, cash flow is not the biggest concern given the dilution (with shares issued to staff). With some 264 million shares outstanding, a $57 stock price implies that shares are valued at around $15 billion.

That, of course, is a giant reduction from peak valuations around the $100 billion mark. With revenues trending around $6 billion here, growing by more than 20%, revenue multiples around 2.5 times look low, even if the company is not posting realistic profits here.

Clearly, such a revenue multiple in relation to sales growth looks like a steal in software land, but the business is not yet really profitable, although it is not posting large losses as well. Investors clearly fear the loss of relevance of the products in the future.

Caution, But Curious

In October, I concluded that I was not joining the team, as a 60% pullback in just about 6 months following has been a dreadful performance. Shares at the time had come down quite a bit as well, but of course had traded at much more demanding sales multiples. I feared the delay on operating leverage from recent dealmaking at the time it was announced for The Browser Company and DX.

These deals depleted cash holdings a bit, and they contributed in a small fashion to the quarterly results. Nonetheless, the pace of growth, a 23% increase in year-over-year sales growth, looked solid, even if slowing down a bit.

It is obvious that all the focus of investors is on AI, notably the threats that it poses to the business, and that is precisely why Atlassian made some M&A actions to better integrate AI offerings into its existing software suite. That risk remains very real in my view to investors.

What Now?

The downfall in the share price of Atlassian has been more pronounced than that of many software peers. This might have to do with the fact that the company focuses heavily on seats, and its clientele largely includes software developers using products like Jira, Confluence, and Service Management, among others.

With these specific roles under threat as well because of AI and self-coding, it is understandable why investors are very fearful. Moreover, the business has a lower consumer component (which, in my view, could accelerate software migration), and unlike many software peers, realistic earnings are not seen.

To offset some pressure, the company announced a 10% reduction in the workforce in March. If these involve equally paid roles (compared to the average), that might actually save around half a billion each year, which would make the business realistically turn profitable, certainly if sales continue to grow.

Frankly, the discussion around terminal value appears priced into the share price right now. Any software business could rather easily become profitable overnight by shedding a lot of staff, and with a 2.5 times sales multiple, it is evident that investors fear shares should be approached with a terminal value in mind.

Given the relentless plunge in the share price, is it concerning that net cash holdings have fallen to half a billion? This makes recent M&A action seem ill-timed, as the additional billion and some would have gone a long way in being able to deliver on the (larger) buyback program here, as little has been communicated on this.

With the third quarter results to be released on the final day of the month, that is a big event that investors are looking forward to. That could be a make-or-break quarter and could rather easily result in large moves higher, given the relentless pessimism here. Potential strong guidance, cost-cutting efforts, and other measures might easily cause some short covering in such a scenario.

Given all this, now might very well become an opportune time to get involved. Realistic earnings are seen here amidst cost-cutting efforts, and while AI concerns prevail, >20% sales growth numbers and a mere 2.5 times sales multiple look compelling enough for a small bet. The focus should be on the word bet here. Given the AI concerns and lack of realistic earnings, the latter of which is to be fixed soon, as sales multiples have collapsed a great deal.

data.title: S&P 500 Rebounds With Iran War Ceasefire, Falling Oil Prices
data.description:
Investors reacted positively to the after-the-market-close news of a ceasefire between the Iran war combatants on 7 April 2026. The S&P 500 (SPX) bounced up 2.5% on the news the following day before proceeding to increase its gains in the remainder of the trading week. By the time the market closed on Friday, 10 April 2026, the index stood at 6,816.89, up a little under 3.6% from its previous week's level.

The news of the ceasefire had an immediate effect on oil prices. Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) had peaked at $117.62 per barrel earlier on 7 April 2026 but plunged as low as $94.62 after the announcement. Crude oil futures went on to spend most of the rest of the week in the mid-to-upper $90 per barrel range.

The drop in oil prices helped shape expectations for how the Federal Reserve will set interest rates in the U.S. While the CME Group's FedWatch Tool continues to foresee no interest rate changes through the end of 2026, it does see a growing chance for a quarter-point rate cut in 2027, with the most likely timing being near the end of 2027-Q2, three months earlier than projected in the preceding week.

In this latest update of the alternative futures chart, the S&P 500 is about four percent below the central trend line of the redzone forecast range we added to the chart after Friday, 20 February 2026.

We had added the redzone forecast range to the chart, fully shown in this version, to compensate for the year-old echoes of two major volatility events, which arise from the dividend futures-based model's use of historical stock prices as the base reference points for its projections of the future. In this case, that was the echoes from February 2025's DeepSeek AI shock and President Trump's April 2025 "Liberation Day" tariff announcements. That redzone forecast range turned out to be ideally suited to use as a counterfactual to measure the impact of the Iran war geopolitical event on U.S. stock prices by indicating where they would reasonably have been in the absence of that event.

The rest is just keeping up with how the flow of news related to the Iran war's developments. Here are the week's market-moving headlines:

Monday, 6 April 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • US employment growth rebounded in March; unemployment rate falls to 4.3%
    • Oil prices rise as US-Israeli war with Iran continues to disrupt supply
      • OPEC+ agrees in principle on theoretical oil output increase
      • US crude premiums climb to record levels as Asia, Europe compete for supply
  • Fed officials not keeping up with oil producer plans, worry about inflation:
    • Fed's Logan says US oil producers unlikely to provide near-term relief for consumers
      • Operators rethink ‘stay the course’ as sustained $100 oil more probable
    • Fed's Goolsbee, Hammack say inflation is flashing 'orange,' or worse
  • Bigger trouble, stimulus developing in China:
    • Trump administration proposes expanding Chinese tech gear crackdown
    • China's Space Pioneer says reusable rocket's maiden flight fails
    • China's Xi urges faster development of new energy system
  • IMF wants BOJ officials to keep turning the screws:
    • IMF urges BOJ to keep raising rates even as Iran war poses new risks
  • ECB officials say oil inflation may force them to drop perfect monetary policies:
    • ECB's Stournaras says euro zone monetary policy will depend on size of energy disruption
  • Wall Street ends higher as traders price in de-escalation, while Iran rejects ceasefire proposal
    • Wall St ends higher as investors parse US-Iran negotiations, threats
      • US to raise Medicare Advantage payments to insurers by 2.48% in 2027, insurance stocks surge

Tuesday, 7 April 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Physical oil prices hit record highs near $150 a barrel as Hormuz crisis worsens
      • From falling U.S. wealth to Indian factory closures, oil shock raises global recession risk
  • Fed officials not so sure what they'll do next with monetary policies, claim they're not bothered by not yet having a new chief official:
    • Goolsbee says stagflationary shock of Iran war puts Fed in a bind
      • Fed's Jefferson sees risks to both employment, inflation
    • Fed's Williams: FOMC leadership not in question amid slow Warsh confirmation
      • Fed's Williams says Middle East war will drive up inflation - Bloomberg
  • Bigger trouble, stimulus developing in China:
    • Chinese pigs fed new menu as Beijing weans farmers off US soy
    • China's central bank buys the most gold in a year as Iran war slashes prices
    • China curbs domestic fuel price hike again to soften impact of surging oil prices
